meta.title: Misinformation and Disinformation Story Tree
meta.description: Three branch stories with independent downstream stages for testing narrative pathways.

storylines.title: Mishearing Path
storylines.description: A fact is misunderstood and then escalates through sharing dynamics.

storylines.title: Manipulation Path
storylines.description: A fact is strategically reframed, amplified, and socially internalized.

storylines.title: Fabrication Path
storylines.description: A fabricated claim is seeded and gains traction before correction catches up.

Vosoughi2018.fullCitation: Vosoughi, Soroush, Sinan Aral, and Deb Roy. "The Spread of True and False News Online." Science, vol. 359, no. 6380, 2018, pp. 1146-1151.
Vosoughi2018.annotationSummary: False information spreads faster, farther, and more broadly than true information due to novelty and emotional engagement.
Vosoughi2018.credibility: Published in Science with large-scale platform data.
Vosoughi2018.projectUse: Explains why spread and amplification stages accelerate quickly.

WardleDerakhshan2017.fullCitation: Wardle, Claire, and Hossein Derakhshan. "Information Disorder: Toward an Interdisciplinary Framework." Council of Europe, 2017.
WardleDerakhshan2017.annotationSummary: Defines misinformation/disinformation framework and the roles of media, technology, and users.
WardleDerakhshan2017.credibility: Council of Europe framework widely used in scholarship.
WardleDerakhshan2017.projectUse: Provides conceptual backbone for all branch transitions.

FerreiraCaceres2022.fullCitation: Ferreira Caceres, M. M., et al. "The Impact of Misinformation on the COVID-19 Pandemic." AIMS Public Health, 2022.
FerreiraCaceres2022.annotationSummary: Documents harmful public-health outcomes linked to misinformation exposure.
FerreiraCaceres2022.credibility: Peer-reviewed public health research based on real events.
FerreiraCaceres2022.projectUse: Grounds the public reaction stage in real-world consequences.

Pew2019.fullCitation: Mitchell, A., et al. "Many Americans Say Made-Up News Is a Critical Problem That Needs to Be Fixed." Pew Research Center, 2019.
Pew2019.annotationSummary: Survey evidence shows high public concern over fake news and trust erosion.
Pew2019.credibility: Pew is a respected data-driven survey organization.
Pew2019.projectUse: Supports trust and social-perception impacts in reaction stages.

DennissLindberg2025.fullCitation: Denniss, Emily, and Rebecca Lindberg. "Social Media and the Spread of Misinformation: Infectious and a Threat to Public Health." Health Promotion International, vol. 40, no. 2, 2025.
DennissLindberg2025.annotationSummary: Misinformation spreads like an infectious process, exploiting emotional triggers and vulnerable groups.
DennissLindberg2025.credibility: Peer-reviewed Oxford journal in health promotion.
DennissLindberg2025.projectUse: Supports viral spread logic in propagation stages.

BrodaStromback2024.fullCitation: Broda, Elena, and Jesper Stromback. "Misinformation, Disinformation, and Fake News: Lessons from an Interdisciplinary, Systematic Literature Review." Annals of the International Communication Association, vol. 48, no. 2, 2024, pp. 139-166.
BrodaStromback2024.annotationSummary: Clarifies distinctions among misinformation, disinformation, and fake news and synthesizes cross-disciplinary findings.
BrodaStromback2024.credibility: Peer-reviewed systematic review spanning multiple fields.
BrodaStromback2024.projectUse: Supports definitions of initial branch divergence.

Lewandowsky2012.fullCitation: Lewandowsky, Stephan, et al. "Misinformation and Its Correction: Continued Influence and Successful Debiasing." Psychological Science in the Public Interest, vol. 13, no. 3, 2012, pp. 106-131.
Lewandowsky2012.annotationSummary: Shows why misinformation persists and why repeated, clear correction is necessary.
Lewandowsky2012.credibility: Foundational peer-reviewed synthesis in cognitive psychology.
Lewandowsky2012.projectUse: Explains why each branch ends with difficult but necessary correction.

Benkler2018.fullCitation: Benkler, Yochai, Robert Faris, and Hal Roberts. Network Propaganda: Manipulation, Disinformation, and Radicalization in American Politics. Oxford University Press, 2018.
Benkler2018.annotationSummary: Demonstrates how media ecosystems and partisan structures reinforce disinformation narratives.
Benkler2018.projectUse: Supports manipulation/fabrication pathways and systemic amplification.

nodes.title: Fact
nodes.definition: Fact is a verifiable statement grounded in reliable evidence and transparent methods. It is the common starting point before the three branch stories diverge.
nodes.example: An official public health bulletin reports verified case data and explains its methodology.
nodes.whyMatters: A trustworthy baseline is necessary to detect later distortion or fabrication.

nodes.title: Mishearing
nodes.definition: Mishearing occurs when people hear only fragments of a true message and reconstruct it inaccurately. This branch is often unintentional at the start.
nodes.example: A person repeats only a headline and leaves out uncertainty in the full report.
nodes.whyMatters: Partial understanding can become sticky and hard to correct once repeated.
nodes.illustrationCaption: This video demonstrates how original ideas are misheard and redrawn until they become unrecognizable, similar to the telephone-game pathway in misinformation.

nodes.title: Manipulation
nodes.definition: Manipulation selectively reframes true information to steer interpretation. It blends fragments of truth with strategic omission or emotional framing.
nodes.example: A real image is paired with misleading context to suggest a false conclusion.
nodes.whyMatters: Manipulation can appear credible while still redirecting public understanding.

nodes.title: Fabrication
nodes.definition: Fabrication is deliberate invention of false content presented as real. This branch begins with intentional disinformation rather than misunderstanding.
nodes.example: A fake article uses invented quotes and branding to mimic a trusted source.
nodes.whyMatters: Fabrication injects fully false narratives into the information environment.
nodes.videoTitle: Professor Dave Debunks Flat Earth Fabrications
nodes.illustrationCaption: Professor Dave proves Eric Dubay uses fabrications in their 200 claims/proofs about the primitive Flat Earth Model.

mediaSources.text: YouTube: Professor Dave Explains – Flat Earth Debunked

nodes.title: Propagation (Mishearing Story)
nodes.definition: In this branch, misheard claims spread through reposts and interpersonal sharing. Repetition increases familiarity and perceived plausibility.
nodes.example: A misunderstood claim is reshared across group chats and short-video platforms in a single news cycle.
nodes.whyMatters: Unintentional distortions can still scale into widespread misinformation.
nodes.illustrationCaption: People that hear the rumor spread it to others within their groups as 'correct' information, interesting stories, etc.

nodes.title: Amplification (Mishearing Story)
nodes.definition: Algorithms and social engagement metrics elevate the misheard narrative to larger audiences. Visibility rises faster than corrective context.
nodes.example: A misunderstood health claim trends after high engagement and recommendation boosts.
nodes.whyMatters: Platform dynamics can intensify accidental misinformation into mass exposure.
nodes.illustrationCaption: This video shows some people misinterpreting "#ClayIsOverParty" as a cancellation against Dream (Minecraft Youtuber). Because so many people were propagating it–the hashtag busted out of its containment and spread to other circles who didn't understand the context (AKA, amplifying it).

nodes.title: Public Reaction (Mishearing Story)
nodes.definition: Audiences respond emotionally and behaviorally to a claim they believe is true. Confusion and mistrust can emerge even without malicious origin.
nodes.example: People delay health actions after believing an incomplete claim shared by friends.
nodes.whyMatters: Even accidental distortion can produce real social and health harm.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Usually, when a mishearing goes viral, there’s three ways the public reacts: propagating it, correcting it, or leaving it alone. Depending on the popularity of the mishearing and the community surrounding it, one way might dominate initially.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Public reaction stage for mishearing story

nodes.title: Correction (Mishearing Story)
nodes.definition: Corrections here focus on restoring missing context and clarifying uncertainty. Repetition and clear replacement narratives are essential.
nodes.example: Experts publish explainer threads that restate the original data with full context.
nodes.whyMatters: Context restoration can reduce harm from misunderstood facts.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Correction can come from the person being misheard or others who witnessed the original context.
nodes.illustrationQuote: YALLLL 13 MILLION VIEWS ON THIS?? I CANNOT KEEP MY SILENCE!!!! I SAID "NICK UH" AND CALLED MY FRIEND THE WRONG NAME 😭 I NEVER SAY THIS WORD IN MY LIFEEE this app is so cooked man
nodes.illustrationQuoteAttribution: — @extraemilyy on X (Feb 23, 2025), correcting the viral mishearing

nodes.title: Propagation (Manipulation Story)
nodes.definition: Manipulated narratives spread through politically aligned or emotionally primed networks. The framing itself becomes the viral unit.
nodes.example: A selectively edited clip is repeatedly posted across partisan pages.
nodes.whyMatters: Strategic framing can spread as fast as outright falsehoods.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Fabrications are initially spread in small circles, garnering enough reactions. Disinfo is often propagated in circles where the original creator knows it'll gain traction and outrage.

nodes.title: Amplification (Manipulation Story)
nodes.definition: Media ecosystems and engagement systems jointly boost manipulated claims. Exposure expands through influencers and networked outlets.
nodes.example: A reframed narrative is repeated across multiple high-traffic channels within hours.
nodes.whyMatters: System-level reinforcement turns framing into dominant public interpretation.
nodes.illustrationCaption: After initial propagation, manipulated content leaves its original bubble and spreads to broader audiences through recommendation and repost networks.

mediaSources.text: YouTube: How disinformation campaigns amplify after initial propagation

nodes.title: Public Reaction (Manipulation Story)
nodes.definition: People react to the reframed version of events rather than underlying evidence. Polarization and institutional distrust can deepen.
nodes.example: A manipulated narrative shifts public attitudes toward policy and expert guidance.
nodes.whyMatters: Manipulated framing can restructure collective understanding and trust.
nodes.videoTitle: Public Reaction: Community Notes and Corrective Spread
nodes.videoCreditText: YouTube: Public reaction to manipulated data
nodes.illustrationCaption: Public reaction often branches into three outcomes: move on, correct the claim, or propagate corrective material further.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Public reaction stage for manipulation story

mediaSources.text: YouTube: Twitter/X Community Notes correcting manipulated content

nodes.title: Correction (Manipulation Story)
nodes.definition: Correction must not only debunk facts but also reintroduce missing context. Debiasing is harder when narrative identity has formed around the manipulation.
nodes.example: Fact-check teams publish side-by-side context comparisons and repeated clarification posts.
nodes.whyMatters: Contextual correction can counter narrative-level distortion over time.
nodes.illustrationQuote: The AP fact-check demonstrates how manipulated claims were corrected by tracing original sources and cross-checking the context of circulated material.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Manipulated data in this incident were debunked by identifying source context and presenting verifiable corrections.

mediaSources.text: AP News: Fact-check – Minneapolis ICE shooting manipulated/fabricated claims

nodes.title: Propagation (Fabrication Story)
nodes.definition: Fabricated claims are intentionally seeded into networks and copied rapidly. Novelty and shock value improve transmission speed.
nodes.example: A fake institutional statement is reposted across anonymous and semi-public channels.
nodes.whyMatters: Intentional falsehoods can spread before verification infrastructures respond.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Fabrications are initially spread in small circles, garnering enough reactions. Disinfo is often propagated in circles where the original creator knows it'll gain traction and outrage.

nodes.title: Amplification (Fabrication Story)
nodes.definition: Once fabricated content attracts engagement, algorithmic recommendation and coordinated sharing elevate visibility. The claim appears mainstream despite false origins.
nodes.example: A fabricated conspiracy post is boosted by high-traffic accounts and enters trending spaces.
nodes.whyMatters: Amplification can normalize fabricated narratives as perceived truth.
nodes.illustrationCaption: After initial propagation, disinformation leaves its original bubble and spreads widely. Here, fabricated election content amplifies from niche posting into the general public.

mediaSources.text: YouTube: How disinformation campaigns amplify after initial propagation

nodes.title: Public Reaction (Fabrication Story)
nodes.definition: Public behavior shifts in response to fully false narratives, often with heightened anxiety or hostility. Trust in institutions can decline sharply.
nodes.example: A fabricated treatment rumor causes panic purchases and avoidance of credible care.
nodes.whyMatters: Fabrication can produce severe downstream harm because the premise is entirely false.
nodes.illustrationQuote: The AP fact-check shows that fabricated claims about the Minneapolis incident were contradicted by available evidence after detailed verification.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Fabricated data about this incident were debunked through thorough analysis – showing how false narratives trigger real public reactions before correction arrives.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Public reaction stage for fabrication story

mediaSources.text: AP News: Fact-check – Minneapolis ICE shooting fabricated data debunked

nodes.title: Correction (Fabrication Story)
nodes.definition: Correction requires explicit debunking, source verification, and repeated counter-messaging. Continued influence effects can persist after exposure to fabricated claims.
nodes.example: Institutions issue formal debunks, evidence links, and repeated updates across multiple channels.
nodes.whyMatters: Rapid, repeated correction is necessary to contain fabricated misinformation damage.
nodes.illustrationCaption: CDC report detailing insufficient data on ivermectin for COVID-19 prevention/treatment, including documented observations of harm from this fabricated health claim.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Screenshot of CDC report on ivermectin and COVID-19

mediaSources.text: CDC: Ivermectin and COVID-19 – Insufficient evidence and harm observations

nodes.title: Propagation
nodes.definition: Whether manipulated or fabricated, misleading content is seeded into partisan and emotionally primed networks. Both strategic framing and invented falsehoods exploit the same propagation channels and social dynamics.
nodes.example: A selectively edited clip and a fabricated institutional statement are both repeatedly posted across aligned pages within the same news cycle.
nodes.whyMatters: Manipulation and fabrication share the same propagation mechanics, spreading as fast as genuine news through familiar social channels.
nodes.illustrationCaption: Manipulated and fabricated content are seeded in small circles that already lean toward the narrative, garnering reactions before breaking into broader audiences.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Propagation stage shared by manipulation and fabrication paths

nodes.title: Amplification
nodes.definition: Algorithmic recommendation, influencer reposts, and media ecosystem reinforcement jointly amplify both manipulated and fabricated content beyond its original circle into mainstream visibility.
nodes.example: A reframed narrative and a fabricated conspiracy post both enter trending spaces after high-traffic accounts boost them within hours.
nodes.whyMatters: System-level amplification normalizes both distorted framing and invented narratives as perceived mainstream truth.
nodes.videoCreditText: YouTube: How disinformation amplifies after initial propagation
nodes.illustrationCaption: After initial propagation, both manipulated and fabricated content leave their original bubble and spread widely through recommendation and repost networks.
nodes.illustrationAlt: Amplification stage shared by manipulation and fabrication paths

mediaSources.text: YouTube: How disinformation campaigns amplify after initial propagation
